intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to Java swaps a flexible, structural type layer built over JavaScript for a mature, enterprise-grade nominal object-oriented programming language. Developers choose this transition to achieve high execution speeds, long-term system maintainability, and access to the deep multi-threaded JVM ecosystem. This shift mandates replacing structural duck-typing habits with explicit, nominal interface and class contract configurations.

diffs.from: Compile-time structural type checks matching shape configurations; supports union types and utility mappings.
diffs.to: Strict nominal static typing verified at compilation; types depend entirely on defined class names and interfaces.

diffs.from: C-style syntax emphasizing flexible functional scopes, object maps, and optional punctuation rules.
diffs.to: Rigid object-oriented patterns where all operational logic must reside within class schemas or record boundaries.

diffs.from: V8 engine heap managed via automatic generational sweeps; application memory limits scale with host tools.
diffs.to: Highly optimized JVM heap managed via production collectors (e.g., G1, ZGC) balancing low-latency targets.

diffs.from: Single-threaded execution loop coordinating micro-tasks, Promise events, and async blocks.
diffs.to: True parallel execution natively leveraging platform threads, thread pools, or lightweight Virtual Threads (Project Loom).

diffs.from: Minimal core requiring packages for typical operations like file system manipulation or advanced networking.
diffs.to: Vast enterprise library providing robust database drivers, networking utilities, and concurrency tools.

examples.title: Strong Interface Implementation
examples.description: Enforcing contracts on data entities using classes.

examples.title: Data Carrying Records
examples.description: Defining immutable data structures with minimal boilerplate.
examples.fromCode:
type User = { readonly id: number; readonly email: string };
const user: User = { id: 101, email: "test@test.com" };

tips.title: Switch to Nominal Validation Models
tips.body: In TypeScript, two types with the same properties are identical. In Java, classes with identical structures are incompatible unless they explicitly implement a shared interface or extend the same parent class.

tips.title: Handle Exceptions via Checked Models
tips.body: Java features checked exceptions that must be formally declared in the method signature or wrapped within `try-catch` structures. This differs significantly from TypeScript, where `catch` clauses default to an unverified `any` type.

tips.title: Utilize Java Records for Objects
tips.body: When translating simple TypeScript types or interface objects used only to move data around, use Java `record` declarations. This eliminates boilerplate by automatically generating getters, `equals()`, and `hashCode()` methods.

tips.title: Adopt Stream Frameworks for Arrays
tips.body: Replace continuous TypeScript list methods like `.map()` and `.filter()` by calling `.stream()` on Java collections, applying operations, and mapping back using collector pipes like `.toList()`.
